original_abstract:
Bench-scale laboratory experiments and column studies were conducted at a waterworks facility to evaluate the removal efficiencies of short- and long-chain per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) using activated carbon filtration and ion exchange (IX) treatment. Across all tested adsorbents, the sorption affinity for short-chain perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs) was markedly lower than that for both long-chain PFAS and short-chain perfluoroalkyl sulfonic acids (PFSAs). In a contaminated groundwater matrix, activated carbon reached breakthrough for the short-chain PFCAs—PFBA and PFPeA—after relatively brief run times of 6,000 and 11,000 bed volumes (BV), respectively, whereas PFCAs with six or more carbons exhibited longer retention. 

Ion exchange media showed slightly improved performance but remained limited for short-chain removal, with breakthrough occurring at 5,000 BV for PFBA, 10,000 BV for PFPeA, and 18,000 BV for PFHxA. While these run times sometimes exceed those of activated carbon, the higher material costs of IX resins prevent a reduction in operating expenses when used in a single-use capacity. However, the economic viability of ion exchange improves when resins are regenerated and reused. Effective regeneration, particularly the elution of long-chain PFAS, necessitates the use of ethanol; conversely, the short-chain PFBA and PFPeA can be successfully extracted from weakly basic ion exchangers without the need for organic solvents.
humanized_abstract:
Short- and long-chain per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) were treated using activated carbon filtration and ion exchange (IX) in bench-scale laboratory experiments and column studies at a waterworks facility. Overall, the short-chain perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs) had lower removal efficiencies compared to the long-chain PFAS and the short-chain perfluoroalkyl sulfonic acids (PFSAs). In a contaminated groundwater, activated carbon had a breakthrough of the short-chain PFCAs, PFBA and PFPeA, after only 6,000 and 11,000 bed volumes (BV), respectively. The PFCAs with 6 or more carbons were retained for a longer time.

Use of Ion Exchange Media A new trial evaluated the effectiveness of ion exchange media. While slightly better than previous work, these media still do not effectively remove short chain compounds. Breakthrough is observed at 5,000 BV for PFBA, 10,000 BV for PFPeA and 18,000 BV for PFHxA. Although greater than some activated carbon service life, the higher cost of ion exchange media does not allow for cost effective operation on a single use basis. However, the ion exchange process can be more economically feasible on a regeneration basis. Regeneration and elution of long chain compounds such as PFOA and PFNA require ethanol. However, short chain compounds such as PFBA and PFPeA can be effectively eluted from weak basic ion exchange media without the use of alcohol.
